Spok Holdings had a solid first quarter of 2025, with results generally in line and still confirming the company's steady, well-managed transition into software-based services. Total revenue was $36.3 million, a 4% increase from the year-ago quarter, fueled by ongoing momentum in its Healthcare Software & Services segment. It was a clean quarter, but one that does not alter the long-term growth story in a fundamental manner.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Total Revenue $36.3 million $34.9 million +4.0%
Adjusted EBITDA $8.2 million $7.5 million +8.9%
Net Income $5.2 million $4.2 million +23.8%
Units in Service 705,000 753,000 -6.4%
ARPU (Average Revenue per Unit) $8.24 $7.89 +4.4%

Quarterly net income was $5.2 million, or $0.25 a share, versus $4.2 million last year—a 23% rise on a percentage basis. Adjusted EBITDA also rose to $8.2 million, an 8.9% rise versus last year. The rises were fueled by a favorable mix shift to higher-margin software and services and continued cost containment. All of this said, however, these gains are more of an extension of ongoing initiatives, as opposed to an inflection point in the history of the company. Barring a pick-up in revenue of an order of magnitude, or other high-impact wins, margins are close to a near-term high.

Software remains the primary driver of growth. Revenue in Healthcare Software & Services grew 9.2% to $17.8 million, with professional and managed services accelerating. Managed services, in particular, were up 183% year-on-year, although it's worth noting the jump was from a small initial figure. Bookings grew modestly to $8.3 million, and backlog was $63.2 million—15.5% higher than a year ago—a sign of having a healthy pipeline of future rollouts. All are good signs, but the size of the overall business still limits how much it can impact near-term performance.

The Wireless Communications segment, which is Spok's bread-and-butter business, is stable but declining slowly. Revenue here was stable at $18.5 million, and units in service decreased 6.4% year-over-year to 705,000. On the positive side, the company did succeed in increasing average revenue per unit by some 4.4%, to $8.24, which served to offset the volume decrease to some extent. The segment still generates good cash flow, but turnaround or growth is not expected from the business unit.

Spok's balance sheet continues to be a source of strength. It ended the quarter with $19.9 million of cash and no debt, leaving the company with a solid balance sheet and flexibility. It also returned $6.4 million to shareholders in the form of dividends and buybacks during the quarter. The $0.3125 quarterly dividend (an almost 8% forward yield) continues to be one of the largest reasons to own the stock, especially for income investors. Yet, that robust yield might also keep Spok from being in a situation to reinvest in larger growth prospects in the future.

Looking ahead, management reaffirmed its full-year 2025 guidance of $134 million to $142 million of revenue and $26 million to $32 million of adjusted EBITDA. The targets imply low top-line growth throughout 2025, and though they anticipate confidence in delivery, they don't anticipate the dramatic change in the growth story.

Valuation: Dividend Yield Anchors the Stock, But Growth Remains Elusive

Trading at $16.94 with ~20.28 million shares outstanding, SPOK has a market cap of ~$345 million. With trailing twelve-month [TTM] net income of $15.9 million, the stock's P/E is 21.66x—not unreasonable, but definitely a sign of a market pricing in consistent earnings and not break-out growth.

More insightful, however, is the company's Enterprise Value [EV], adjusted for its $19.9 million of cash and no debt, of approximately $325 million. To this, we can add TTM EBITDA of $24.9 million, and we derive an EV/EBITDA multiple of ~13.05x—a reasonable price for a cash-generative, recurring revenue business, but not compelling enough to indicate the business is dramatically undervalued, particularly with modest growth expectations.

What's keeping the stock in check today is its strong dividend yield of ~7.4%, based on an annual payout of $1.25 per share. A major attraction for income investors, that's especially noteworthy considering Spok's healthy balance sheet and consistent free cash flow. But it also reflects a strategic trade-off: with so much capital going out to shareholders, reinvestment in long-term growth—especially in the emerging software business—may be constrained.

To assess where SPOK might be headed, I ran a simple DCF model. Nothing fancy—just some conservative estimates based on their guidance, historical trends, and a bit of judgment. I used a 9% discount rate across all three cases. That feels fair for a small-cap healthcare tech company with no debt and decent margins, but modest growth. Free cash flow conversion is assumed at 70% of EBITDA, which matches their historic run rate.

Let’s start with the key input assumptions I used for the DCF.

DCF Input Table

Assumption Pessimistic Case Base Case Optimistic Case
Revenue CAGR (2025-2031)

0%

1.75%

5.5%
Starting EBITDA (2025)

$23M

 $25.5M $30M
FCF as % of EBITDA

70%

 70% 70%
Terminal Growth Rate

1.5%

 2% 2.5%
Discount Rate (WACC)

9%

 9% 9%

The base case scenario assumes that they hit their 2025 guidance and manage to grow steadily. The optimistic case expects the software segment to gain more traction, while the pessimistic one assumes the legacy business keeps dragging and software doesn’t scale much.

Now, in terms of terminal growth rate — that’s just my best guess at what the business looks like after 2031. I used a terminal growth rate of 1.5% in the pessimistic case, 2% in base, and 2.5% in the optimistic one. Those numbers are basically in line with inflation plus a bit of real growth, mostly from software. Just assuming Spok stays steady and keeps generating decent cash flow into the future. Seems fair for a niche player with sticky clients, but limited upside opportunities.

Now let’s look at the projected free cash flow for each scenario.

DCF Forecast (2025-2031)

Year Pessimistic FCF Base Case FCF Optimistic FCF
2025 16.1 17.9 21
2026 16.1 18.2 22.1
2027 16.1 18.6 23.3
2028 16.1 19 24.5
2029 16.1 19.3 25.9
2030 16.1 19.7 27.3
2031 16.1 20.1 28.8

Source: Author's calculations

In the pessimistic case, FCF is flat for 7 years—no real growth. That’s basically what happens if the software push fails to scale. The base case assumes modest year-over-year improvement, while the optimistic view prices in stronger operating leverage and top-line growth from software.

After projecting the cash flows and applying the terminal value using the respective growth rates, here's what the model gives us in terms of share value.

DCF Output Table

Scenario Estimated Value/Share Upside/Downside
Pessimistic $13 ~-23%
Base Case $16.75 ~0%
Optimistic $19.50 ~15%

Source: Author's calculations

Let’s break this down:

  • Pessimistic Case: Assumes flat to slightly lower revenue with EBITDA falling to $23 million as wireless segment revenue falls more sharply than software gains. Fair value in a pessimistic-case scenario would fall to ~$13.00/share, suggesting downside risk of ~-23% and highlighting sensitivity to even moderate margin or top-line weakness.
  • Base Case: Assumes 1.75% yearly revenue growth and stable EBITDA of $25.5 million. In this scenario, Spok is reasonably valued at approximately $16.75/share, which implies that the current price already embodies its fundamental core and projectable cash flows.
  • Optimistic Case: Presumes successful scaling of the software business, with revenue growth inflecting higher at 5.5% per annum and EBITDA growing to ~$30 million. Under this more optimistic case, fair value increases to ~$19.50/share, suggesting upside potential of ~15% if execution significantly exceeds expectations. Spok looks fairly range-bound on valuation across the three cases. Dividend cushion and balance sheet flexibility are provided, but there is as yet not a lot in the numbers to justify multiple expansion without a step-change in software growth or margin acceleration.

Across the three cases, Spok looks fairly range-bound on valuation. With the dividend as a cushion and the balance sheet as a source of flexibility, there is presently not much in the numbers to sustain a multiple expansion unless there was a step-change in software growth or margin acceleration.

Catalysts

Spok's continued momentum in bookings and contract renewals offers increasing proof that its strategic transition into healthcare software is building traction. In Q1 2025 alone, 22 six-figure contracts were signed by the company—a good indication that large hospital systems are not just taking on Spok's offerings, but also committing for longer periods. Further success in winning or renewing large deals could validate the transition of the business model and ultimately warrant a re-rating of the stock.

The firm's high dividend yield, now hovering close to 7.4%, remains a draw for income investors. At a payout ratio of approximately 60% of 2024 EPS, the dividend looks sustainable over the near term. In a world where reliable yield is hard to find, particularly as interest rates normalize, Spok's dividend profile provides structural support to the stock irrespective of weak growth.

More generally, from an industry standpoint, healthcare IT is experiencing secular tailwinds as hospitals modernize communications and compliance becomes more stringent. Spok offerings—like Care Connect and Spok Mobile—map directly into this trend of digitization and clinical efficiency. Regulatory reforms or patient safety initiatives that boost demand for fault-tolerant, real-time communications in clinical settings could be organic growth levers.

There is also latent optionality over strategic alternatives. If Spok continues to be inexpensive compared to its recurring software revenue and balance sheet, it would likely make it a takeover candidate for larger healthcare IT participants seeking hospital communications system exposure. Strategic partnerships with EHR vendors or telcos could otherwise fuel product growth and create synergies.

Risks

In spite of solid execution thus far, Spok's core wireless business continues to be in structural decline. Paging is a dying industry, and although average revenue per unit increased in Q1, wireless units in service declined another 6.4% year over year. As this trend picks up speed, topline and margin pressure will increase unless software growth offsets more meaningfully. Wireless revenue resilience is a risk that can't simply be diversified away from.

Spok is subject to growing competition in healthcare messaging within the industry. Larger, more integrated platforms—and newer cloud- or Wi-Fi-based systems—are a risk to customer hold and pricing power. Hospitals are now more likely to look for integrated digital health ecosystems, and vendors such as Vocera, PerfectServe, and even broadline IT vendors can replace Spok if it fails to innovate at a faster pace and stay relevant.

Customer concentration is another underappreciated risk. A large portion of Spok's revenue is generated by a relatively small number of large hospital systems. The loss of even one of these large customers—or the inability to execute on a complicated implementation—would have disproportionate financial and reputational consequences. Execution risk also rises as Spok ramps up professional services and moves into more subscription-oriented contracts.

Financially, the company's liberal dividend policy is a double-edged sword. Although the yield is appealing, it takes a deep bite out of free cash flow, limiting the potential for internal reinvestment. Should EBITDA or earnings fall even slightly, the company could be compelled to rethink its payout, and this would serve to dampen investor sentiment and support shares.

Lastly, healthcare IT spending is cyclically and politically exposed. Hospital budgets can swing with fluctuations in Medicare/Medicaid reimbursement rates, federal or state policy, or macroeconomic trends. A slowdown in funding, delay of hospital capital projects, or regulatory flux can delay purchases or push out rollout cancellations. Furthermore, although interest rates now appear to be stabilizing, any additional rise might compress valuations for dividend-paying tech companies like Spok.

Conclusion

Spok Holdings keeps on performing well in its strategic shift away from legacy paging toward healthcare-oriented software, with Q1 2025 results reaffirming operational stability and consistent growth in recurring, higher-margin revenue streams. The company boasts a healthy balance sheet with no debt and a strong $19.9 million cash position, while still returning capital to shareholders in the form of a handsome dividend—currently yielding approximately 7.4% at a stock price of $16.94.

But with paging business secularly declining, growth still growing modestly, and the software business too small to be material to overall revenue or margins yet, long-term potential remains limited. Competitive pressures, customer concentration, and execution risk are still significant headwinds.

My discounted cash flow [DCF] model calculates a base-case fair value of around $16.75/share, roughly in line with the current price. Though an optimistic scenario can support up to $19.50/share (+15% upside), a pessimistic scenario supports downside to $13.00/share (–23%). The market seems to be rationally valuing Spok's steady-state cash flows and high dividend, but not assigning premium value to future growth optionality. Amidst such dynamics, I am assigning a HOLD rating. Valuation today presents neutral risk-reward, and though income investors can still take advantage of the dividend, a cleaner inflection in software-driven growth would be needed to warrant a more positive view.
